coredata.dc:title: Construction of Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots by Aggregation-Induced emission
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kou, Xinyue
Li, Li
Mei, Qian
Dong, Wen-Fei
Wang, Yucai
coredata.dc:description: Aggregation-induced emission luminogens (AIEgens) have garnered significant attention because of their outstanding photophysical characteristics. AIEgens are used in fluorescence imaging, sensors, tumor treatment, and other related fields. However, the synthese of these AIEgens are relatively complicated and requires expensive raw materials. These drawbacks limit their applications and development to a certain extent. In this study, using cheap and convenient materials, we developed a new type of carbon dots (O-CDs) using a one-step solvothermal method, which has the potential to become a new AIEgen. O-CDs exhibit different fluorescence colors in different solvents, and they exist as monomers in ethylic acid and, ethanol alcohol, etc., exhibiting blue fluorescence. After adding water, the fluorescence of O-CDs gradually turns orange red, because the internal rotation of the disulfide bond molecules is restricted and the AIE effect occurs. Using the unique AIE performance of O-CDs, we fabricated an anti-counterfeiting luminous ink, that can be used for encryption in the reversible double switch mode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-Induced Emission
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Luminescence ink
Reversible dual-fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: O-CDs-based double switch mode luminescence ink
Application of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2020YFC2004600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21803075, 91959112, 81902166, 62027825, and 82172077 ), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 2020SYHZ0041), the Instrument Developing Project of Chinese Academy of Science (YJKYYQ20200038), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019683), the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326), and the Science and Technology Department of Jinan City (2018GXRC016).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: O-CDs’ formation, appearance and fluorescent effect. (a) Preparation of O-CDs and the formation of aggregates. (b) Schematic diagram of O-CDs’ two-switch-mode luminescence.

Characterization of O-CDs. (a) TEM image of the O-CDs. (b) Size distribution of O-CDs. (c) High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of the O-CDs, inset: FFT diffraction pattern of carbon lattice. (d) XRD patterns of the O-CDs. (e) Raman spectra of the O-CDs. (f) FT-IR spectrum of the O-CDs, inset: FT-IR spectrum of OPD and DTSA.
XPS spectrum high-resolution C 1 s (a), O 1 s (b), N 1 s (c), S 2p (d) spectra of O-CDs.
(a) 1H NMR and (b) 13C NMR spectra of the O-CDs in DMSO‑d 6.

Absorption and emission properties of O-CDs. (a) UV − vis absorption (blue line), FL excitation (EX) (black line), and emission (EM) (red line) spectra of as-prepared O-CDs solution. (b) FL emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths of the as-prepared O-CDs. (c) UV − vis absorption (blue line), FL excitation (Ex) (black line), and emission (Em) (red line) spectra of the O-CDs powder. (d) FL emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths of O-CDs powder.

Emission spectra of O-CDs with different solvent. (a) Photographs of the as-prepared O-CDs solution with the volume ratio of water varies from 10% to 90% under sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet radiation. (b, c) FL emission spectra of the as-prepared O-CDs solution with the volume ratio of water varies. (d, e) Trend of the as-prepared O-CDs solutions’ fluorescence intensity at 474 nm and 583 nm.

(a) PL emission spectra of the O-CDs in solutions with different polarities (acetic acid, DMSO, DMF, ethanol and water). (b) Photographs of the O-CDs solutions under sunlight (up) and under 365 nm radiation (down).

Presentation and principle of O-CDs’ reversible dual-fluorescence. (a) Photographs of filter papers with different treatment under variable irradiation. (b) Schematic showing mechanism of O-CDs as a two-switch-mode luminescence ink (The off-white rectangle represents filter paper, and the crossed wavy lines represent filter paper fibers).
Application of O-CDs ink. (a) Encryption effect without adding water; (b) Encryption effect after adding water.
Schematic illustration of the preparation and the fluorescence emission of O-CDs.

head.ce:title: Construction of Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots by Aggregation-Induced emission

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhengzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aggregation-induced emission luminogens (AIEgens) have garnered significant attention because of their outstanding photophysical characteristics. AIEgens are used in fluorescence imaging, sensors, tumor treatment, and other related fields. However, the synthese of these AIEgens are relatively complicated and requires expensive raw materials. These drawbacks limit their applications and development to a certain extent. In this study, using cheap and convenient materials, we developed a new type of carbon dots (O-CDs) using a one-step solvothermal method, which has the potential to become a new AIEgen. O-CDs exhibit different fluorescence colors in different solvents, and they exist as monomers in ethylic acid and, ethanol alcohol, etc., exhibiting blue fluorescence. After adding water, the fluorescence of O-CDs gradually turns orange red, because the internal rotation of the disulfide bond molecules is restricted and the AIE effect occurs. Using the unique AIE performance of O-CDs, we fabricated an anti-counterfeiting luminous ink, that can be used for encryption in the reversible double switch mode.

ce:section.ce:para: A novel family of organic fluorogens was discovered by Tang et al. in 2001, which is diametrically opposed to traditional organic dyes that suffer from aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [1–3]. When molecularly dispersed in excellent solvent, these new fluorogens exhibit extremely weak fluorescence, and when present in poor solvents or solids, they exhibit high-intensity fluorescence, which is the known as fluorogens with aggregation-induced emission (AIEgens) [4–6]. AIEgens are gaining considerable attention because of their outstanding photophysical characteristics, such as strong photobleaching resistance and high solid-state emission [7]. Bioimaging [8], organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) [9], sensing [10,11], and anti-cancer therapy [12,13] have only a few applications.

Organic compounds [14], metal particles [15], and polymers [16] have been discovered to produce AIEgens. By developing molecules utilizing the twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT) method [17], several AIEgens exhibit variable emission intensities, as well as adjustable emission wavelength. However, most of these AIEgens require costly precursors or complicated synthesis, making them impractical for application. Consequently, sustainable AIEgens with adjustable emission wavelengths will be a big step forward from current AIEgens.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted attention as sensing probes in recent years because of their outstanding photoluminescent (PL) characteristics, biocompatibility, and low toxicity [18–21]. Unfortunately, similar to common organic fluorescent dyes, most CDs are affected by the ACQ effect due to strong π-π interactions in their graphitizing cores [22]. To develop CDs-based AIEgens, we utilized o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) to produce hydrophobic N, S-doped CDs, named O-CDs, which do not suffer from ACQ and have a double switch mode luminescence between the dissolved emission of blue and the AIE of orange. After calculation, the mass ratio of the purified CDs to the raw material was 70%, and the reaction yield was high compared to those of previous techniques [23,24]. O-CDs exhibited different fluorescence colors in different solvents. When the O-CDs powder was dissolved in ethanol or acetic acid, they exist as monomers and exhibiting bright blue fluorescence. When dissolved in DMF, the O-CDs solution appear light red, owing to the superposition of blue and orange; when dissolved in water, the O-CDs solution exhibits only orange fluorescence under 365 nm or 254 nm irradiation because the internal rotation of the disulfide bond molecules are restricted and the AIE effect occurs (Scheme 1 ).

Various control experiments were designed to verify the luminescence mechanism of O-CDs and the relationship between the dispersion state and fluorescence (e.g., p-aminoazobenzene (PAB) and citric acid (CA) were used to eliminate the influence of disulfide bonds on the structure and fluorescence properties). As an attempt for practical applications, the prepared O-CDs solution was added to the marker pen for anti-counterfeiting and encryption tests, and based on this, a reversible double switch mode luminescence ink was developed.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). Nano ZS/ZEN 3690 (Malvern, UK) was used to investigate the particle size distribution and surface potential of the O-CDs. Raman spectra were recorded on a DXR Raman microscope using the 780 nm excitation line of a diode laser in laser power density at 40 kW/cm2. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra was obtained using a Kratos Axis Ultra system with a monochromatic Al K X-ray source. The chemical composition and valence states of constituent elements were analyzed by XPS. The ultraviolet–visible (UV/vis) absorption was measured using a UV/vis absorption spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 300 Scan, USA). The UV–Vis measurements were obtained in the wavelength range 200–700 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet AVATAR 360 FTIR spectrophotometer, in the frequency range 4000–400 cm−1 with 32 scans. The fluorescence emission spectra of the O-CDs were obtained using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker AVANCE NMR spectrometer (600 MHz) using Deuterium-labeled DMSO‑d 6 as the solvent.

OPD, DTSA, and acetic acid were purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA), p-Aminoazobenzene (PAB), ethylenediamine (EDA) and ethanol alcohol were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. All reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and have high purity available, without further purification.

172.8 mg of OPD and 272 mg of DTSA were added into 40 mL acetic acid. After sonication for 30 min, the mixture was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (80 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After the solvothermal treatment, the reactor was automatically cooled to room temperature, and the prepared O-CDs solution was added to 200 mL of deionized water. Solid powder formed immediately in the solution. The solution was sonicated for 5 min and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Repeat the appeal step three times until the solvent and remaining raw materials are removed, and then dry the resulting solid powder in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C.

O-CDs were prepared from DTSA and OPD in acetic acid solution through a one-pot solvothermal process, followed by simple purification (Fig. 1 a). In the preparation of O-CDs, acetic acid as the solvent is the most critical condition. When water was added to the prepared translucent O-CDs solution, the solution gradually became a turbid liquid, and the blue emission decreased. With an increase in water content, a red halo appears in the solution. After purification and drying, O-CDs powder with orange solid fluorescence was obtained under 365 nm UV irradiation. Notably, under 254 nm UV, the O-CDs powder still emitted orange, and the O-CDs dispersion had almost no fluorescence (Fig. 1b).

The as-prepared O-CDs were analyzed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, XPS, FT-IR spectra, and NMR spectroscopy. TEM images (Fig. 2 a) show that the CDs are regular spherical, evenly distributed, and the single particle size is approximately 3–4 nm, which is consistent with the test results of the Malvin particle sizer (Fig. 2b). The high-resolution TEM image clearly proves that the fine structure of CDs has crystal distances of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystallographic faces of graphite carbon (Fig. 2c) [25,26]. The XRD spectra show that the O-CDs have an unobvious peak near 25°, which is attributed to their graphitized carbon core. The peak near 41° further verifies the crystal distances of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2d) [27]. The Raman spectrum reveals two peaks at 1340 and 1590 cm−1, which represent the D band and G band, respectively referring to the areas of disordered sp3 carbon atoms and sp2 carbon networks in the O-CDs’ frameworks (Fig. 2e). The calculated intensity ratio ID/IG was 0.185, indicating that the content of sp2 graphite carbon atoms in O-CDs was relatively higher than that of disordered sp3 carbon atoms [28,29]. FT-IR analysis (Fig. 2f) confirmed the presence of functional groups or chemical bonds on the surface of the O-CDs, such as methylene (2877 cm−1), S-H (2658 cm−1), amide carbonyl (1682 cm−1), C-N (1589 cm−1), C = C (1462 cm−1), aromatic C − NH (1263 cm−1), C-O (1150 cm−1), C-S (694 cm−1) and S-S (492 cm−1). In contrast, FT-IR analysis of the raw materials reveals that OPD contains an amino group (3410 cm−1), and DTSA contains a hydroxyl group (3068 cm−1). After amidation and carbonization, the hydrophilic groups in O-CDs disappear, resulting in the enhancement of the hydrophobicity [30,31].

The chemical structure of the O-CDs was further investigated using XPS and NMR spectroscopy. The full spectra of XPS analysis of the O-CDs (Fig.S1) clearly shows four peaks at 164.08, 283.79, 398.29, and 532.79 eV, which are attributed to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The atomic ratios of C, N, O, and S elements were calculated as 77.71%, 2.24%, 12.09%, and 7.96%. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 3 a) confirms the existence of C-C/C = C (284.78 eV), C-N (286.48 eV) and C = O/C = N (288.88 eV) bonds [32]. Hence, the high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s (Fig. 3b) exhibits two peaks at 533.27 and 531.95 eV, which are attributed to C = O and C-O functional groups [33]. The N1s peaks (Fig. 3c) at 401.78, 400.38 and 398.76 eV indicate that nitrogen exists mostly in the form of graphitic-N (N in graphene basal plane), pyrrolic-N (N in 5-membered ring) and pyridinic-N (N in 6-membered ring) [34]. The S2p band (Fig. 3d) contains two peaks at 163.68 and 164.98 eV, which are assigned to S-C and S-S respectively. These data prove that N and S were successfully doped into O-CDs.

NMR spectroscopy (1H and 13C) was also used to characterize the properties of the O-CDs. Fig. 4 a shows the 1H spectra of the O-CDs. The signals in the range of 1.05–2.51, 3.43–3.46, and 7.33–7.54 ppm, were assigned as –CH3, –CH2 and –CH, respectively. Additionally, the –OH signal of the carboxylic acid in the DTSA is replaced by an amide signal (8.03 ppm) in the O-CDs, which is consistent with FT-IR analysis. The precise structure was determined by 13C NMR spectra (Fig. 4b). Because sp3 and sp2 carbons have different chemical environments, their 13C NMR signals differ. The former ranged from 8 to 80 ppm and the latter from 90 to 180 ppm. The 13C NMR spectra of the O-CDs revealed five chemical shift peaks at 126.01, 128.11, 133.30, 138.97, and 167.65 ppm. The signals from 124.75 to 138.79 ppm are caused by aromatic sp2 carbons, and the signal at 167.65 corresponds to carboxyl/amide groups. Combined with these characterization results, it can be inferred that the synthesis of O-CDs is mainly composed of sp2 graphite carbon atoms, pyridine nitrogen atoms and disulfide bonds, which correspond to the doping of C, N, O, and S atoms. This also explains the hydrophobicity and optical properties of O-CDs.

The O-CDs exhibited different optical properties in solution and powder forms. Fig. 5 a shows the UV–vis spectra of the O-CDs in the solution. There are two clear peaks at 300 and 360 nm, which are probably due to the π–π* transitions of the C = C in the O-CDs. The best emission peak was observed at 474 nm, with the corresponding excitation at 380 nm (Fig. 5b). Fig. 5c shows the UV absorption spectrum of the O-CDs in the powder. The O-CDs powder exhibits absorption in the entire visible light region (from 200 to 700 nm), which is likely due to the π–π* transitions of the surface states containing C = N/C = O, C–O and C − S structures. The fluorescence emission of the O-CDs powder at various excitation wavelengths is shown in Fig. 5d. Fluorescence emission depended on the excitation wavelength. When the excitation emission was 410 nm, the O-CDs powder exhibited the strongest emission at 583 nm, displaying bright orange fluorescence.

Fig. 6 a shows a photograph of the as-prepared O-CDs solution under white light and 365 nm ultraviolet radiation, where the volume ratio of water varied from 10% to 90% (the other solvent was acetic acid). With the addition of water, the color of the solution changed from dark brown to light brown. When the volume ratio of water exceeded 50%, the orange powder began to separate from the as-prepared O-CDs. The original clear solution became an opaque, turbid liquid with suspended particles. Under UV radiation at 365 nm, the solution with a water addition ratio of less than 40% exhibited blue fluorescence. When the water addition ratio was higher than 50%, the opaque liquid containing suspended particles emitted pink to orange fluorescence.

Fig. 6b and 6c show the changes in the fluorescence spectra of the blue and orange regions of the O-CDs solution with different proportions of water content. With the increase in water content (from 10% to 90%), the fluorescence intensity at 474 nm decreased sharply and finally almost disappeared. In contrast, the fluorescence peak appeared in the orange region after the water content reached 40%, and the fluorescence intensity increased with the amount of water. When the water content reached 90%, the final fluorescence peak was observed at 583 nm. Fig. 6d and 6e intuitively reflect the changes in fluorescence intensity at 474 and 583 nm, respectively, under different water ratios.

Next, the effects of solvents on AIE were investigated. The O-CDs were prepared in a 1 mg/mL solution with different solvents (acetic acid, DMSO, DMF, ethanol, and water), the emission spectra were tested (Fig. 7 a), and the fluorescence color was observed (Fig. 7b). When acetic acid (solvent polarity ε = 6.2) was used as the reference, ethanol (ε = 4.3) caused a slight blue shift in the fluorescence of the solution, resulting an indigo color. In DMSO (ε = 7.2) or in DMF (ε = 6.4) solution, the main fluorescence peak remains unchanged at 474 nm, but an additional shoulder peak appears at 600 nm, which makes the fluorescence pink (superimposed by blue and orange). In water (ε = 10.2), the initial blue fluorescence peak almost disappeared, and a new main peak formed at 600 nm, resulting in bright orange fluorescence. One possible reason for this is the effect of solvent polarity on AIE, that is, highly polar solvents (relative to acetic acid) are more likely to form J-aggregation of CDs, resulting in a red shift of the fluorescence.

It is well known that, the key factors for preparing O-CDs are the use of DTSA + OPD as the raw material and acetic acid as the solvent. Thus, to explore the luminescence mechanism of O-CDs, five control experiments were conducted and divided into three groups (see the Supporting Information for the detailed preparation method).
In the first group, DTSA was removed or replaced with either CA or PAB. The prepared CDs were named F-CDs, C-CDs, and P-CDs, respectively.

As shown in Fig. S2, the prepared F-CDs original solution emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV and did not exhibit fluorescence under 254 nm UV. When 10% and 50% volumes of water were added, the fluorescence color changed from light blue to dark blue at 365 nm UV, and purple fluorescence was generated at 254 nm UV. For the C-CDs, under 365 nm UV light, the fluorescence gradually changed from light green to dark green with increasing of water content. However, under 254 nm irradiation, the C-CDs solution exhibited only weak blue fluorescence. For P-CDs, the solution exhibited no fluorescence under 365 nm or 254 nm irradiation, regardless of the addition of water. The first group of experiments confirmed that CDs without DTSA as a raw material cannot cause a fluorescence red shift by adding water.

The second group involves replacing acetic acid with ethanol as a solvent under the original experimental conditions, named E-CDs. Fig. S3 shows that under 365 nm light, the solution emits bright yellow fluorescence, and the color becomes slightly lighter after adding water, whereas under 254 nm light, the E-CDs solution is brown without fluorescence. In other words, when the acetic acid solvent was replaced, the phenomenon of fluorescence change with water content also disappeared.

The third group replace OPD as a carbon source with EDA, named A-CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, under 365 nm light, the blue fluorescence of the A-CD solution gradually changed to pink with the addition of water, and under 254 nm light, the fluorescence of the solution changed from grape color to orange. This phenomenon is similar to that observed for the original O-CDs solution.

The above experiments demonstrate that disulfide bonds and acetic acid play important roles in the formation of O-CDs. Here, a possible luminescence mechanism was proposed. In solution, O-CDs are dispersed and exist in the form of monomers. The graphitized core of O-CDs is a fluorescent chromophore, and the rotatable symmetrical heterocycle around the disulfide bond has no effect on fluorescence emission. Therefore, the O-CDs solution exhibited bright blue fluorescence, which is consistent with most traditional central nuclear luminescent CDs. When the O-CDs monomer meets water, owing to the excellent hydrophobicity, the CDs gather close to each other, triggering π - π accumulation to overlap with each other, and finally form a J-aggregate shape. This aggregation causes the graphitized core to trigger π - π accumulation, which causes the ACQ to extinguish the blue fluorescence. Meanwhile, axisymmetric heterocyclic compounds are limited by the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) of the rotationally symmetric heterocyclic around the disulfide bond, triggering AIE and bringing orange fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: O-CDs-based double switch mode luminescence ink
ce:section.ce:para: Next, O-CDs were used to develop a double switch luminous ink. Fig. 8 a shows the results of the subsequent treatment with different solvents after wetting the filter paper with the O-CDs solution. The letter “S” refers to the untreated filter paper, “I” refers to the filter paper wetted by O-CDs solution, “B” refers to the filter paper wetted by O-CDs solution and treated with additional water, “E” refers to adding ethanol to the treatment method of “B”, and “T” refers to adding water again according to the treatment method of letter “E”. Under white light, “I” is almost the color of the filter paper. Under 365 and 254 nm UV lamps, it emits blue-white and dark blue fluorescence, respectively. After adding water and drying, the letter “B” turns blue under 365 nm ultraviolet light. And under 254 nm ultraviolet light, it appears in the form of orange fluorescence, indicating that the O-CDs on the filter paper contains these two substances (O-CDs monomer, and O-CDs aggregate). By adding ethanol and drying, the O-CDs solution is restored to the same optical properties as the O-CDs monomer, such as the letter “E”. Similarly, after adding water again, the orange fluorescence can be turned on again, such as the letter “T”, indicating that the two-color mode is reversible.

Fig. 8b shows a schematic of the dual fluorescent ink. The crossed S-shaped curve represents the fiber of the filter paper. Owing to the limitations of filter paper fibers, blue dots are O-CDs dispersed on the filter paper in the form of monomers. According to a previous analysis, the O-CDs monomer can emit blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light, but cannot emit fluorescence under 254 nm ultraviolet light. Once water was added, some O-CDs monomers gathered on the surface of the filter paper while other O-CDs monomers still adhered to the filter paper fibers. Therefore, under 365 nm ultraviolet light, the O-CDs monomer in the filter paper and the aggregate on the surface emit blue and orange fluorescence, respectively, and are finally superimposed into light pink fluorescence. The O-CDs monomer will not be excited under 254 nm ultraviolet light; therefore, it can only exhibit orange fluorescence. If ethanol is added, these aggregates will re dissolve into monomers and enter the filter paper; thus, the fluorescent discoloration process is reversible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, and O-CDs solution was used to develop a portable anti-counterfeiting and encryption tool. The solution was added to the blank marker as ink to obtain an encrypted fluorescent pen (OMP). After writing letters on the filter paper with the OMP and a commercial highlighter pen (CHP), the encryption effects of OMP and CHP are compared.

As shown in Fig. 9 a, under white light, the letters had almost no color, which is the same as the original color of the filter paper. The letters drawn by CHP exhibited stable and clear blue fluorescence under ultraviolet rays at 365 and 254 nm. Letters drawn by the OMP exhibit two optical properties under the same ultraviolet light. Under 365 nm ultraviolet light, the letters exhibit blue and white fluorescence, and under 254 nm ultraviolet light, the letters exhibit dark blue fluorescence. Next, add a layer of water to the “I” and “e” of the two groups of letters. The letters drawn with CHP do not lead to fluorescence changes, and the letters “I” and “E” will not be further encrypted. Under 254 nm ultraviolet light, the letters “I” and “E” drawn by OMP turn orange fluorescence (Fig. 9b), which is significantly different from other letters. Under 365 nm ultraviolet light, these two letters exhibit blue and white fluorescence, which are slightly different from other letters. In general, under the combined action of UV lamp and water, the letters “I” and “E” written by OMP are double encrypted, which is better than the existing fluorescent pen.

In brief, we developed O-CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method using inexpensive and convenient raw materials. The O-CDs were comprehensively analyzed using various characterization methods to determine their structure and properties. When O-CDs are dispersed in acetic acid, ethanol, or other solvents, they exist as monomers and emit blue light under UV irradiation. Owing to the amidation and carbonization treatment in the preparation process, the hydrophilic groups in the raw materials disappeared, the O-CDs exhibited excellent hydrophobic properties. Therefore, with the addition of water, the prepared O-CDs will precipitate and exist in the form of aggregation. Because the internal rotation of disulfide bond molecules is limited, the surface of the O-CDs results in AIE and produces orange emission under ultraviolet light. When monomers and aggregates exist simultaneously, they produce blue and orange fluorescence, respectively. These characteristics enable the use of O-CDs to produce anti-counterfeiting luminous ink with a reversible double switch encryption mode. This encryption effect has been proven to be significantly better than that of commercial fluorescent pens.

Xinyue Kou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Li Li: Software, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Qian Mei: Methodology, Data curation. Wen-Fei Dong: Methodology, Data curation, Validation. Yucai Wang: Supervision, Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2020YFC2004600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21803075, 91959112, 81902166, 62027825, and 82172077), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 2020SYHZ0041), the Instrument Developing Project of Chinese Academy of Science (YJKYYQ20200038), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019683), the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326), and the Science and Technology Department of Jinan City (2018GXRC016).
